ghreadme 0.1.0

    • Create a GIF for your GitHub README
    • Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
  • Changelog

Create a GIF for your GitHub README

create-gif.Rmd

GitHub profile READMEs support animated GIFs, making it easy to show a short introduction that plays automatically when someone visits your profile. This vignette walks through using who_am_i_gif() to generate that GIF and embed it in your README.

Prerequisites

who_am_i_gif() requires three packages that are not installed with ghreadme by default:

install.packages(c("camcorder", "gifski", "ggplot2"))

camcorder records each ggplot frame to a temporary PNG, and gifski stitches those PNGs into the final GIF. ggplot2 renders the text as plot frames.

Create the GIF

Call who_am_i_gif() with the same information you would pass to who_am_i(). The output argument controls where the GIF is saved — set it to a path inside your profile repository:

library(ghreadme)

who_am_i_gif(
  name   = "Martin",
  likes  = "#rstats and data visualization.",
  learn  = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",
  work   = "R package development tools.",
  collab = "#rstats packages for data science.",
  output = "intro.gif"
)

The function prints each message as a cumulative frame on a dark background, mimicking console output. After the last frame there is a 4-second hold before the GIF loops, giving readers time to read the final line.

Adjust timing and size

Argument Default Effect
frame_duration 1.5 Seconds each new-line frame is shown
width 800 GIF width in pixels
height 400 GIF height in pixels 
who_am_i_gif(
  name           = "Martin",
  likes          = "#rstats and data visualization.",
  learn          = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",
  work           = "R package development tools.",
  collab         = "#rstats packages for data science.",
  output         = "intro.gif",
  frame_duration = 2,
  width          = 900,
  height         = 450
)

Omit the connect message

Set connect = FALSE to drop the final “Want to connect?” line:

who_am_i_gif(
  name    = "Martin",
  likes   = "#rstats and data visualization.",
  learn   = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",
  work    = "R package development tools.",
  collab  = "#rstats packages for data science.",
  connect = FALSE,
  output  = "intro.gif"
)

Embed the GIF in your README

In your profile repository, reference the GIF at the top of README.Rmd (before the badge links) so it plays when visitors land on your profile:

---
title: "Hi there 👋"
output: github_document
---



![](intro.gif)

Knit README.Rmd to regenerate README.md, then commit both files:

git add README.Rmd README.md intro.gif
git commit -m "update profile README"
git push

Your profile page will display the animated introduction automatically.

Full workflow 

library(ghreadme)

# 1. Generate the GIF into your profile repo
who_am_i_gif(
  name   = "Martin",
  likes   = "#rstats and data visualization.",
  learn   = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",
  work    = "R package development tools.",
  collab = "#rstats packages for data science.",
  output = "path/to/mjfrigaard/intro.gif"
)

# 2. Generate badge markdown for the README body
gh_badges(
  username = "mjfrigaard",
  badge    = c("details", "commit_lang", "stats"),
  theme    = "dark"
)

# 3. Generate Stack Overflow badge HTML
so_rep(username = "martin-frigaard", user_id = "4926446")

On this page

Developed by Martin Frigaard.
